title: Discovery Weekend

jokes.text: Mike McLintock identifying himself as 'BuzzFeed magazine — print edition'
jokes.setup: A reporter introduces themselves at what appears to be a press moment
jokes.punchline: Mike specifies 'print edition' of BuzzFeed — a digital-native outlet that famously has no print edition — revealing his hopeless obsolescence
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical character joke. BuzzFeed having a 'print edition' is an absurdist impossibility that simultaneously nails Mike's cluelessness about the media landscape. The specificity ('print edition') is what elevates it from a simple 'Mike is dumb' joke to something more precise and clever.

jokes.text: Dan: 'I'm not her brother, and I never was. Except for that one year.'
jokes.setup: Someone asks how Dan and Amy can marry given they're stepsiblings
jokes.punchline: Dan firmly denies being her brother — then immediately admits he was, for exactly one year
jokes.explanation: Exceptional structure. The denial 'I'm not her brother, and I never was' is delivered as a firm, clean rebuttal — the comic pivot 'Except for that one year' lands with perfect timing because it completely undermines the denial while raising more questions than it answers. The self-interrupting structure is close to flawless. Highly quotable and deeply weird in exactly the right way.

jokes.text: Amy tells Dan she's pregnant and 'not asking anything of you, literally nothing' — then Dan says 'Okay, goodnight' and Amy says 'Goodnight'
jokes.setup: Amy drops a bombshell pregnancy announcement and explicitly says she wants nothing from Dan
jokes.punchline: Both immediately say goodnight as if it were a normal farewell — the mutual emotional avoidance is the entire joke
jokes.explanation: The joke IS the deflation. Everything about Dan and Amy's broken dynamic is captured in two words: 'Goodnight.' The fact that this is how a pregnancy announcement ends between these two characters is both funny and quietly devastating. Perfect economy — the writers trust the silence entirely.

jokes.text: Selina watches a political opponent on TV and says 'Oh God, he looks good' with evident disgust
jokes.setup: Selina is shown footage of a rival candidate giving an inspiring speech about the country being a 'shining beacon of hope'
jokes.punchline: Her response ignores the content entirely — she's only threatened by his looks
jokes.explanation: A solid character beat — Selina's vanity and political anxiety compressed into one line. It's familiar Veep territory but executed cleanly.

jokes.text: Roger to Selina during the square dance: 'I would have invited my wife, but she's a squirter, and that dress doesn't look like it's been Scotch-guarded.'
jokes.setup: Roger greets Selina warmly at the square dance, appearing to make small talk about why his wife isn't there
jokes.punchline: The reason is a graphic sexual overshare about his wife's physiology and concerns about the fabric of Selina's dress
jokes.explanation: Shock value with genuine construction behind it. The setup — 'I would have invited my wife, but...' — mimics a perfectly normal social explanation. The pivot to the sexual overshare is genuinely unexpected. The Scotch-guard detail elevates it from gross to absurdly specific, which is what transforms shock comedy into good comedy. Selina's response 'Ever the charmer' is a perfect straight-man button.

jokes.text: Selina: 'For that amount of money to my campaign, I'll do-si-do for his do-si-dough.'
jokes.setup: Ben explains they need to keep dancing and schmoozing to get Felix's check
jokes.punchline: Selina's wordplay on 'do-si-do' and 'dough' (money) lands as a rare self-aware one-liner from her
jokes.explanation: The pun is groan-worthy in the best possible way — 'do-si-dough' is stupid and perfect. The joke works on two levels: the wordplay itself, and the revelation that even in crisis Selina is pleased with herself for making it. Great economy.

jokes.text: Selina watching Felix's event: 'No wonder the rest of the world hates us.'
jokes.setup: Felix introduces his 'Discovery Weekend' as a place to 'build bridges made of ideas' and innovate entrepreneurial solutions
jokes.punchline: Selina's withering four-word summary of the entire Davos-style elite gathering
jokes.explanation: Punchy and accurate. The joke functions as both satire of the Aspen Ideas Festival/TED Talk industrial complex and a character beat for Selina — she despises these people even as she courts them. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Selina says she hates being called a 'lightweight.' Staff member responds: 'Why? You're so skinny.'
jokes.setup: Selina expresses genuine political frustration about Felix always thinking of her as 'a lightweight'
jokes.punchline: The staff member mishears the political term as a comment about her weight, giving her an unintended compliment
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection joke with a double meaning that works in both directions. The structure is near-perfect: the political anxiety of the setup makes the literal physical interpretation a genuine surprise. 'N... Oh. Yeah.' from Selina accepting the compliment is a nice button.

jokes.text: Gary's tactic: repeat the last two sentences Felix says back to him. Selina calls it 'idiotic.' Gary immediately demonstrates by repeating her exact words back.
jokes.setup: Ben explains Felix's insecurity means you must repeat his last sentences back to him; Selina dismisses it as idiotic
jokes.punchline: Gary, already in sycophant mode, immediately repeats Selina's dismissal back to her — proving both the tactic's power and his own Pavlovian obedience
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed joke. The premise (repeat Felix's words) is introduced. Selina dismisses it. Gary — who exists to mirror and validate Selina — immediately does the exact thing Selina is dismissing, but to Selina. It's a perfect joke about both Felix's vanity and Gary's nature. The escalation of Gary then doing it to Felix pays off the setup further. Structure is exceptional.

jokes.text: Selina tells Felix the only reason she's running for president is to attend his event. Felix says 'Really? I feel like you should have a better reason than that.'
jokes.setup: Selina is flattering Felix by saying his event is the only reason she's running
jokes.punchline: Felix — the man whose approval she desperately needs — gently suggests she should have a better reason, undercutting the flattery
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the reversal — Selina's desperate flattery backfires and gets a mild rebuke. Solid situational comedy.

jokes.text: Selina's aide repeating Felix's sentences back to him — 'It has been a delight, and you shall see me later. Thank you very much.'
jokes.setup: The 'repeat the last two sentences' tactic has been established
jokes.punchline: Gary executes it flawlessly and Felix is visibly pleased — the idiotic tactic works perfectly, which is the joke
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the established gag. The humor is in seeing the stupid tactic validated — Felix's delight confirms both his narcissism and the writers' satirical point about powerful men.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Isn't it astounding that the next President of the United States is being chosen by a closeted ex-record producer?'
jokes.setup: Selina watching Felix hold court at his event
jokes.punchline: The compressed absurdity of how American political power actually works, delivered as irritated observation
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire with excellent economy. Every word is doing work: 'astounding' (faux-naïve), 'closeted' (dangerous overshare), 'ex-record producer' (the specific absurdity of the donor class). Works as satire and as character voice.

jokes.text: Response to Selina's observation: 'The Electoral College hasn't exactly been hitting it out of the park.'
jokes.setup: Selina marvels that this one donor is choosing the president
jokes.punchline: Ben's dry observation that the official system isn't doing better — both validating her point and expanding the satire
jokes.explanation: Solid Ben line — deadpan and politically pointed. The baseball metaphor ('hitting it out of the park') applied to constitutional democracy is a nice deflating touch.

jokes.text: On Felix's young companion: 'He'll look older after Felix switches blood with him.'
jokes.setup: Someone notes that Felix's companion seems very young
jokes.punchline: Ben implies Felix is a vampire-like figure who literally harvests youth — a dark riff on elite blood transfusion trends
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. The blood-switching joke references the real Silicon Valley trend of young-blood transfusions for the ultra-wealthy while simultaneously implying vampiric predation. The logic is perfectly inverted — the companion will look OLDER after Felix takes his youth. Specific, dark, and extremely quotable.

jokes.text: 'Felix Wade's sexuality is the worst best-kept secret since Clyde Tolson's hysterical pregnancy.'
jokes.setup: The team is discussing Felix's closeted status
jokes.punchline: The comparison to Clyde Tolson (J. Edgar Hoover's widely-known companion) combined with the absurdist 'hysterical pregnancy' creates a layered historical joke
jokes.explanation: Outstanding line. Clyde Tolson was J. Edgar Hoover's presumed lover — invoking him for a 'worst best-kept secret' joke is both historically specific and perfectly apt. But the addition of 'hysterical pregnancy' (an archaic, impossible male condition) is absurdist genius — it layers impossibility on top of the obvious secret. This requires knowledge to fully land but rewards the informed viewer enormously. Among the best pure jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Selina: 'The man lives in a vast invisible closet. — Sounds amazing.'
jokes.setup: Ben describes Felix's closeted life
jokes.punchline: Selina hears 'vast invisible closet' and immediately pivots to literal closet envy — revealing her priorities are fashion-based
jokes.explanation: Two words carry enormous comedic weight: 'Sounds amazing.' The joke is entirely about what Selina does and doesn't hear — she mishears a political metaphor as a literal closet and wants one. It's a perfect Selina moment: vain, shallow, and oblivious. Character integration is exceptional — this joke is impossible coming from anyone else.

jokes.text: Gary having a stomach upset, clearly having witnessed/encountered something disturbing, then immediately being sworn to secrecy — played entirely in reaction
jokes.setup: Gary is found looking ill and makes vague reference to having 'ate' something
jokes.punchline: The visible distress and immediate plea for secrecy — implying Gary has stumbled onto something he cannot unsee (context suggests he saw Amy vomiting/purging)
jokes.explanation: Works better visually than on the page. The transcript-only version loses the physical comedy. Still registers as a comedic beat setting up the Amy bulimia reveal.

jokes.text: Mike's email is Mike29748@aol.com. When called out, he clarifies: 'It's Mike53729. — Still @aol though, right? — Until I die.'
jokes.setup: Dan mocks Mike about new media by revealing his AOL email address
jokes.punchline: Mike doesn't object to the AOL part — only corrects the numbers. His 'Until I die' commitment to AOL is the final punctuation
jokes.explanation: Three-beat joke with escalating precision. Beat 1: The AOL email (shocking). Beat 2: Mike only corrects the numbers, not the domain (escalation). Beat 3: 'Until I die' — a statement of genuine existential commitment to obsolescence. The joke is about technological death denial and it's beautifully built. 'Until I die' is exceptionally quotable.

jokes.text: Ben tells Selina 'Amy's bulimic' like it's useful intelligence; Selina responds 'Well, it's about time.'
jokes.setup: Ben presents Amy's bulimia as tactical information
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'Well, it's about time' implies she's been waiting for Amy to develop an eating disorder — casually monstrous
jokes.explanation: Selina being delighted by Amy's eating disorder is peak character comedy — it reveals her total inability to register other people's suffering unless it serves her. 'Well, it's about time' implies she's been expecting and perhaps wishing for this. Casually monstrous and deeply funny.

jokes.text: Selina on Amy's bulimia: 'She might want to consider a little more purging, a little less bingeing. God, that girl can't do anything right.'
jokes.setup: The team discusses Amy's bulimia
jokes.punchline: Selina critiques the quality of Amy's eating disorder as if it's a performance review — Amy can't even binge-purge correctly
jokes.explanation: Among the best jokes in the episode. 'God, that girl can't do anything right' applied to bulimia is a masterpiece of character comedy — it takes Selina's management-speak criticism of Amy and applies it to a medical condition. The implication that Amy should improve her bulimia technique is so dark it loops back to funny. Only Selina could say this line.

jokes.text: Ben tells Amy she has 'some vomit on your mustache'
jokes.setup: The team has just been discussing Amy's bulimia; she approaches
jokes.punchline: Ben's casual, factual notification — treating vomit on the face as a minor grooming note — is the joke
jokes.explanation: The 'mustache' detail is specific and humiliating. The deadpan delivery normalizes something disgusting. Amy's response 'What the Rocky Mountain fuck?!' is a great button.

jokes.text: Amy: 'What the Rocky Mountain fuck?!'
jokes.setup: Amy has just been told she has vomit on her mustache, is at an upscale Aspen resort
jokes.punchline: The location-specific expletive — 'Rocky Mountain fuck' — is Amy's regional variation on the profanity, absurdly place-specific
jokes.explanation: Brilliant location-specific profanity. 'Rocky Mountain fuck' is original, specific, and perfectly absurd. The joke is that someone at their lowest has still absorbed enough local color to customize their outburst. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Selina to Tom: 'Gosh, I didn't see a TED talk for fake folksiness on the schedule.'
jokes.setup: Tom James arrives with warm, folksy charm
jokes.punchline: Selina cuts through his affect with a specific institutional insult — his folksiness is so performative it belongs at TED
jokes.explanation: Sharp insult with good political specificity. 'Fake folksiness' as a TED genre is an accurate and funny satirical observation. The line works as both an attack on Tom and a swipe at TED culture.

jokes.text: Tom responds to 'what time is that TED talk?' with '4:30.'
jokes.setup: Selina sarcastically asks when the TED talk on fake folksiness is scheduled
jokes.punchline: Tom gives a straight answer as if she made a sincere inquiry, implying he genuinely has a 4:30 slot
jokes.explanation: One-word button that perfectly caps the exchange. Tom refusing to take the bait by treating the insult as a genuine question is both characteristically smooth and quietly devastating — he genuinely has a talk scheduled.

jokes.text: Selina: 'That makes me moister than an oyster.' / Kent: 'Plus or minus four points.' / Selina: 'OK, still pretty moist.'
jokes.setup: Kent reads a poll showing Selina is beating Tom by 18 points in Iowa
jokes.punchline: Selina's sexual/food metaphor for political excitement ('moister than an oyster') followed by Kent's immediate statistical caveat and Selina adjusting her enthusiasm accordingly
jokes.explanation: Three-part joke that builds beautifully. 'Moister than an oyster' is an inspired vulgarity — rhyming, food-based, sexual, and completely specific to Selina's register. Kent's 'plus or minus four points' is a perfect straight-man deflation. Selina's 'OK, still pretty moist' is the ideal button — she adjusts her enthusiasm by exactly four points of moisture. Highly quotable and showcases the ensemble timing perfectly.

jokes.text: Selina on Tom's Deputy Chief of Staff: 'Good Lord, what's up with Frigid Von Pole-Up-Her-Ass?'
jokes.setup: Tom's stone-faced aide appears
jokes.punchline: Selina's invented aristocratic German name for a humorless woman — 'Frigid Von Pole-Up-Her-Ass'
jokes.explanation: Inventive character naming is one of Veep's signature comic modes, and this is a strong example. The Germanic noble-sounding construction ('Von Pole-Up-Her-Ass') is absurdly specific. The joke pays off harder when we learn this is 'his Amy' — setting up the episode's parallel structure.

jokes.text: Tom: 'She's basically my Amy.' / Selina: 'Well, my Amy has bulimia.' / Tom: 'No one likes a braggart, Selina.'
jokes.setup: Tom describes his aide as 'basically my Amy'
jokes.punchline: Selina brags about Amy's bulimia as a competitive differentiator; Tom calls her out for bragging
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best joke constructions. The callback to Amy's bulimia is weaponized as social capital — Selina is BRAGGING about her staffer's eating disorder as if it's a trophy. Tom's 'No one likes a braggart, Selina' treats the boast as merely impolite rather than monstrous, which is the perfect straight-man response. The joke works because it takes seriously the premise that a bulimia diagnosis is a competitive advantage. Exceptional character integration — the dynamic between these two characters makes it land twice as hard.

jokes.text: Selina on an all-female ticket: 'An all-female ticket? I don't think so. The American people work hard for a living, OK? They don't need that kind of bullshit.'
jokes.setup: An aide suggests an all-female ticket could be historic and powerful
jokes.punchline: Selina — a woman, a feminist-coded politician — dismisses the idea as 'bullshit' that hardworking Americans don't deserve, revealing her cynicism about identity politics cuts both ways
jokes.explanation: A genuinely pointed piece of political satire. Selina's cynicism about female solidarity is both character-perfect (she uses feminism when convenient) and a sharp observation about political calculation. The logic — Americans 'work hard for a living' therefore don't need a female ticket — is magnificently backwards, implying that a female ticket would be a burden on the hardworking. This is Veep at its satirical sharpest.

jokes.text: Selina and Kemi bond over Senator Isles grabbing their asses. Selina: 'He used to say that mine was like a candy apple.' — then learns he's under investigation — 'Oh, yeah, right. 'Cause that's bad now.'
jokes.setup: Kemi confirms Senator Isles grabbed her ass; Selina happily volunteers her own experience as bonding material
jokes.punchline: 'Cause that's bad now' — Selina's perfectly timed recalibration as she remembers sexual harassment is no longer acceptable
jokes.explanation: 'Cause that's bad now' is exceptional — it captures an entire generation's recalibration in three words. Selina's transition from sharing a warm harassment memory to remembering it's disqualifying is both funny and culturally sharp. The 'candy apple' specificity is a bonus detail that makes the original anecdote funnier while making her eventual correction more hypocritical.

jokes.text: Selina 'accidentally' runs into Tom outside: 'Wow, that is so incredible! I did not see you there.' — Tom: 'Well, that's funny that you sprinted around the entire deck to just bump into me sight unseen.'
jokes.setup: Selina has clearly engineered a 'chance' encounter with Tom
jokes.punchline: Tom's precise anatomical description of her sprint ('around the entire deck') deflates her pretense entirely
jokes.explanation: Solid character comedy — Selina's transparent scheming versus Tom's amused clarity. The joke is about the collapse of pretense and the familiar dynamic between these two characters.

jokes.text: Tom: 'One thing I've learned in politics, a lot of the best stuff happens afterhours.' / Selina: 'Oh, is that so, Captain Morgan?'
jokes.setup: Tom is mansplaining political strategy to Selina with faux wisdom
jokes.punchline: 'Captain Morgan' — a pirate/rum brand nickname implying he's drunk and/or a swashbuckling rogue who stays out late
jokes.explanation: Sharp retort. 'Captain Morgan' implies both his alpha-male performance and the fact that he spent the night drinking. Good economy.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Thank you so much for man-telling me that.' / Tom: 'I believe the word you're fumbling for is mansplaining.' / Selina: 'Man... OK... splain...' — then immediately cuts away
jokes.setup: Selina tries to use 'mansplaining' as an attack on Tom
jokes.punchline: Tom corrects her on the word — forcing her to concede the vocabulary point to the man she's accusing of condescension. Her trailing off ('Man... OK... splain...') shows her losing the thread
jokes.explanation: A clever structural reversal: the person trying to weaponize 'mansplaining' gets the word wrong and has the correct term explained to them by the man she's accusing. Tom's correction compounds the insult by being accurate. The fact that she fumbled for it as 'man-telling' makes it funnier — she understands the concept but doesn't know the word.

jokes.text: Staff thinks a photo of Tom's schedule is a photo of his aide. 'Of his Amy? His who? His Amy. Ma'am, I don't see it... No ma'am, I got a picture of his schedule.'
jokes.setup: Selina is excited about a photo she thinks incriminates Tom somehow
jokes.punchline: She's been imagining the aide photo was something more; it's just his schedule. The callback to 'his Amy' adds another layer
jokes.explanation: A functional confusion joke that plays on the audience's investment in the 'his Amy' running thread. The reveal (just a schedule) deflates nicely.

jokes.text: 'Amy, seriously, I know I rarely say this, but that was really...' [cuts off before compliment is delivered]
jokes.setup: Selina appears to be about to give Amy a genuine compliment for getting Tom's schedule
jokes.punchline: The 'O... K.' cut — Selina never completes the compliment. The audience realizes she physically cannot bring herself to praise Amy
jokes.explanation: A structural joke that lives in the gap — the incomplete compliment IS the joke. Works excellently on rewatch when you know what's coming (or not coming).

jokes.text: Amy is implied to have eaten a second breakfast before her visible breakfast. 'Did you see what she had for breakfast? — And I bet she had a breakfast before that breakfast. — There is no doubt about that.'
jokes.setup: The team continues mocking Amy's eating disorder
jokes.punchline: The speculation that Amy pre-ate before the visible meal is delivered with clinical certainty
jokes.explanation: A solid extension of the eating disorder thread. 'There is no doubt about that' delivered with certainty about a second pre-breakfast is a nice deadpan beat.

jokes.text: Congressman Ryan: 'Within five years, I want America... to put a man on Ellen DeGeneres.'
jokes.setup: A panel is asking Congressman Ryan about his big new ideas; the setup mimics the JFK moon speech ('put a man on the moon')
jokes.punchline: The completion replaces 'the moon' with 'Ellen DeGeneres' — a non-sequitur that is both homophobic-adjacent in its absurdism and targeting a specific cultural figure
jokes.explanation: Solid absurdist joke that subverts a famous political phrase. The pause before the reveal is structurally well-placed. The 'Up top!' reaction from Richard reveals he put Jonah up to it — and that he found his own joke funny. The follow-up 'She's a lesbian' explanation is the overstated button that kills the magic (intentionally).

jokes.text: Richard explains the Ellen joke to the panel: 'She's a lesbian.' — Panel responds: 'No, we get it.' — 'Right.' — 'We get the joke.' — 'OK.'
jokes.setup: Richard made the Ellen joke; it landed
jokes.punchline: Richard feels compelled to explain 'She's a lesbian' — ruining the joke mid-applause; the panel's 'We get it' / 'We get the joke' is the mortified straight-man response
jokes.explanation: Richard explaining his own joke is a classic character beat — his inability to read the room is one of his defining traits. The panel's increasing frustration ('We get the joke. OK.') is perfectly rendered cringe comedy.

jokes.text: Jonah's sexual harassment roster: 'Emma Gray' is called 'Schnozz.' 'Monster Tits and Monster Tits, Jr.' for Congresswomen Troy and Vasconi. 'All I did was express genuine concern they had bras big enough to handle all that meat. At the Congressional Prayer Breakfast.'
jokes.setup: Teddy lists Jonah's accusers, asking who he's traumatized
jokes.punchline: Each name Teddy gives produces a worse nickname and worse excuse from Jonah; the Prayer Breakfast detail is the escalating topper
jokes.explanation: Escalating list of Jonah's transgressions builds well. Each item is worse than the last: 'Schnozz' is merely offensive, 'Monster Tits and Monster Tits, Jr.' is grotesque, and the Prayer Breakfast context makes the bra-concern even more obscene. Jonah's 'I mean, he's a man too, Teddy' about Jesus is a genuinely surprising button. Excellent character integration — only Jonah has this specific brand of oblivious misogyny.

jokes.text: 'But all I did there was express some genuine concern that they had bras big enough to handle all that meat. At the Congressional Prayer Breakfast.'
jokes.setup: Jonah defending his behavior toward Congresswomen Troy and Vasconi
jokes.punchline: 'At the Congressional Prayer Breakfast' — the location tag makes an already egregious statement catastrophically worse
jokes.explanation: The location escalation is superb. The sentence is offensive but manageable — then 'At the Congressional Prayer Breakfast' lands like a bomb. The sanctimony of the venue makes the behavior more spectacular. Jonah's follow-up ('it was exactly what Jesus would have done') is a great button.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'I mean, he's a man too, Teddy.' (about Jesus endorsing his concern over bra sizes)
jokes.setup: Jonah justifies his Prayer Breakfast comments as 'what Jesus would have done'
jokes.punchline: His appeal to Jesus-as-fellow-man as theological cover for sexual harassment is magnificently stupid
jokes.explanation: A standout Jonah line. The logic is perfectly constructed: Jonah invokes Jesus to justify harassment at a Prayer Breakfast, and when challenged, his rebuttal is 'He's a man too' — the worst possible theological defense. The economy is exceptional: one short sentence contains an entire character worldview. Among the most quotable lines in the episode.

jokes.text: Teddy's advice: 'Tell 'em you've been chemically castrated. It's very easy to lie about. Believe me, nobody checks.'
jokes.setup: Teddy is trying to manage Jonah's sexual harassment accusations
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'nobody checks' regarding chemical castration — delivered as practical crisis communications advice — is the joke
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: the absurdity of using chemical castration as a PR lie, and the reassurance 'nobody checks' — which implies Teddy has considered or used this before. 'Believe me, nobody checks' is delivered with the weary authority of experience. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: After interacting with Jonah, someone hands out Purell: 'Well, that was a waste of time. Here's some Purell. I need, like, a tub of this stuff.'
jokes.setup: Dan has just dealt with Jonah
jokes.punchline: The Purell distribution — as if Jonah is a physical contamination hazard — and the need for a 'tub' of it
jokes.explanation: A reliable visual gag — treating Jonah as a biohazard. Familiar Veep territory but executed cleanly.

jokes.text: Selina on Tom and Felix: 'He cannot spend another second with Felix without me jammed in between 'em like the cross-piece on an Eiffel Tower three-way. MMF. The Devil's threesome.'
jokes.setup: Selina needs to interrupt Tom and Felix's bonding
jokes.punchline: The 'Eiffel Tower' sexual position reference applied to a political situation, with Ben clarifying 'MMF' and adding 'The Devil's threesome'
jokes.explanation: Inventive and specific. Using a threesome position as a political strategy metaphor is very Veep. The fact that Ben feels compelled to clarify 'MMF' (as opposed to the presumably preferred MFM) and then editorialize 'The Devil's threesome' is an excellent character beat — even Ben has opinions on threesome configurations.

jokes.text: Selina on Dan being sent to charm Felix: 'In current gay parlance, Dan presents somewhere between a wolf and an otter, some would call a frost otter.'
jokes.setup: The team discusses whether Dan can attract Felix's attention
jokes.punchline: Ben's deployment of precise gay bear community taxonomy to classify Dan — 'frost otter' — delivered with total authority
jokes.explanation: The joke derives enormous power from Ben — the oldest, most cynical, most conservative-coded character — deploying highly specific gay subcultural vocabulary with complete confidence. The term 'frost otter' sounds invented but fits the taxonomy perfectly, and Ben's certainty makes it funnier. This is pure character comedy: the gap between who Ben is and what he knows is the entire joke. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Felix dismisses Dan: 'Fly away, please, we're busy.' / Tom: 'Bye, Dan.' / Felix: 'Nice try, Grandma, but you aged out of Felix's demo back in the 20th century.'
jokes.setup: Dan attempts to flirt/charm Felix to steal him from Tom
jokes.punchline: Felix calls Dan 'Grandma' and specifies his demo cutoff was 'the 20th century' — a precise and devastating age-based rejection
jokes.explanation: The 'Grandma' insult for a man is unexpected, and '20th century' as a demo cutoff is both specific and funny. Dan's vanity being weaponized through his aging is a nice ongoing thread.

jokes.text: Felix tells Dan: 'You may want to start coloring that gray. Just a thought.'
jokes.setup: Felix has already rejected Dan; this is a parting shot
jokes.punchline: Offering grooming advice as a consolation after a humiliation — 'Just a thought' — is perfectly calibrated condescension
jokes.explanation: Good button. The gentleness of 'Just a thought' after a devastating rejection is an excellent tone — Felix is still trying to be helpful as he destroys Dan's ego.

jokes.text: Ben advises Amy to 'try laxatives' for her bulimia: 'I'll tell you why. It saves your teeth.'
jokes.setup: Ben tells Amy 'we all know' about her bulimia
jokes.punchline: Rather than sympathy, Ben offers practical harm-reduction advice about laxatives — his rationale being tooth preservation
jokes.explanation: The specificity of tooth-saving as the rationale is both medically grounded (purging does damage teeth) and completely wrong in its priority. Ben treating bulimia as a logistics problem with an engineering solution is perfectly in character — he's the world-weary pragmatist who optimizes everything. 'I'll tell you why' sets up the dental twist beautifully.

jokes.text: Amy reveals she's pregnant. Selina reacts: 'Can you imagine? / That's not funny. / Now I'm gonna be sick.'
jokes.setup: After extended speculation and mockery about Amy's bulimia, she reveals she's pregnant
jokes.punchline: Selina's escalating horror — 'Can you imagine? / That's not funny. / Now I'm gonna be sick.' — treats the pregnancy as worse than anything that's come before
jokes.explanation: The reveal lands with good structural weight — we've been in the bulimia joke for a while and the pregnancy recontextualizes it. Selina's three-beat reaction ('Can you imagine?' then 'That's not funny' as a self-correction, then her own sympathetic nausea) is well-constructed.

jokes.text: Selina: 'What, were you wearing a full-length mirror?' / Aide: 'That's tough, but fair.'
jokes.setup: Selina is horrified that Dan slept with Amy
jokes.punchline: The 'full-length mirror' insult implies Dan only sleeps with women who look like him — and since Dan is conventionally attractive, the insult runs both ways
jokes.explanation: Elegant insult construction. 'Were you wearing a full-length mirror?' implies both Dan's narcissism (he only sees himself) and questions Amy's choice (is she Dan?). The aide's 'That's tough, but fair' button is excellent — validating the cruelty while acknowledging its accuracy is a perfect reaction beat.

jokes.text: Selina: 'God Almighty, shoulda had you fixed years ago.'
jokes.setup: Selina is reacting to Amy's pregnancy
jokes.punchline: Describing Amy's reproductive capacity in veterinary terms ('had you fixed') — treating her staffer like a pet
jokes.explanation: Selina treating Amy as property (an animal to be spayed) is a perfect encapsulation of the Selina/Amy dynamic — simultaneously dehumanizing and oddly affectionate (she says 'years ago,' implying long ownership). The casual past-tense regret ('shoulda had you fixed') is inspired. Among the most quotable lines in the episode.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Listen, I did not spend my entire life defending a woman's right to choose for you to choose this.'
jokes.setup: Amy is considering keeping the baby
jokes.punchline: Selina's pro-choice politics are revealed to extend only to choices she approves of — the logical contradiction delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: The single best joke in the episode and arguably one of the best political jokes in all of Veep. The construction is perfect: the first half is a genuine statement of feminist principle, building to what appears to be support — then the pivot 'for you to choose THIS' inverts the entire premise. Selina's entire political identity (feminist, pro-choice) is revealed as a costume she wears until it conflicts with her wishes. The line is both a perfect character joke and a devastating political observation about performative feminism. Comparable to anchor-level craft. Quotability is off the chart.

jokes.text: Felix overhears and says: 'I can't count the number of times I've wanted to say the same thing.' / 'Do you know that there's one girl that worked in my office who had four kids. What was she, a possum?'
jokes.setup: Felix has overheard Selina's rant about Amy's pregnancy
jokes.punchline: Felix's validation and his escalation — 'What was she, a possum?' — reveals he shares Selina's contempt for reproduction
jokes.explanation: Felix's escalation (four kids = possum) is a good absurdist beat. The fact that he bonds with Selina over reproductive contempt also efficiently establishes their relationship dynamic. 'Actually Korean, but that's very funny' from the aide is a nice button.

jokes.text: Aide on Felix's possum joke: 'Actually Korean, but that's very funny.'
jokes.setup: Felix asks 'What was she, a possum?' about a prolific mother
jokes.punchline: The aide corrects the implied ethnicity comparison (possum → Korean) then immediately endorses the joke as 'very funny'
jokes.explanation: The aide's correction implies Felix's possum joke had a racist undertone the aide felt obligated to address — but then endorses it as 'very funny' anyway. The sycophantic capitulation after the correction is a good character beat.

jokes.text: Felix tells Selina he's been calling her 'a bit of a lightweight' for years. Selina, using the repeat-back tactic: 'Well, O... Yes, I mean, I can be a bit of a... A lightweight. You said it, not me!'
jokes.setup: Felix finally voices the 'lightweight' critique to Selina's face, expecting her to push back
jokes.punchline: Selina, using the repeat-back tactic, accidentally agrees she's a lightweight — and then catches herself with 'You said it, not me!'
jokes.explanation: Beautiful triple callback. The 'lightweight' insult from earlier, combined with the repeat-back tactic, produces a moment where Selina's strategy defeats her — she repeats the insult back to confirm it. 'You said it, not me!' is a perfect panic recovery that makes it worse. The structural elegance of two running gags colliding is excellent.

jokes.text: The 'repeat the last two sentences' technique used at full scale with Felix: 'That's an incredible anecdote!' (Selina echoing Felix's enthusiasm about his own anecdote)
jokes.setup: Felix recounts his record-industry story
jokes.punchline: Selina's technique produces robotic over-enthusiasm — 'That's an incredible anecdote!' — that Felix completely buys
jokes.explanation: The running gag continues — Selina deploying the technique in a one-on-one context. 'Incredible anecdote' is particularly good — it's what no one would naturally say but is precisely the phrasing you'd land on if mirroring.

jokes.text: Jonah's apology to Amanda: 'I'm sorry, Amanda, if I behaved in a way that made you uncomfortable or inappropriate. All right? God! And you should try smiling more.'
jokes.setup: Teddy has coached Jonah through a prepared, heartfelt apology
jokes.punchline: The apology is technically passable until 'And you should try smiling more' — a mansplaining non-sequitur that invalidates the entire apology in one clause
jokes.explanation: The 'All right? God!' in the middle of the apology already signals it's not going well — the exasperation undercuts the contrition. But 'And you should try smiling more' is the perfect Jonah self-destruct. The structure builds a legitimate apology then demolishes it with one appended clause. Exceptional character integration — Jonah is constitutionally incapable of not making it worse.

jokes.text: Amanda produces her OWN non-disclosure agreement: 'As far as the law is concerned, we never dated.'
jokes.setup: Teddy presents Amanda with a standard NDA to sign regarding her accusations
jokes.punchline: Amanda has her own NDA — but it's to make Jonah legally deny they dated, because she doesn't want the world to know
jokes.explanation: Superb structural reversal. The setup establishes one power dynamic (Jonah's team trying to silence the accuser) and the punchline completely inverts it — she wants HIM silenced because being associated with Jonah is reputationally damaging. This is both a great joke and sharp commentary on the nature of public humiliation. The misdirection is perfectly executed.

jokes.text: Jonah refuses to sign: 'You are a stone-cold, neck-down hottie. I want the whole world to know we dated.'
jokes.setup: Amanda wants Jonah to legally deny their connection
jokes.punchline: Jonah's refusal is based on pride — she's 'neck-down' attractive (implying her face is not), and he's proud of the association
jokes.explanation: 'Stone-cold, neck-down hottie' is a very specific, very Jonah insult disguised as a compliment — simultaneously aggressive, specific, and revealing of his worldview. His desire to publicize the 'relationship' for status reasons escalates the comedy further.

jokes.text: Amanda: 'We had one working lunch, and we split the bill.' / Jonah: 'I split the bill on all my dates.' / Amanda: 'Why would I pay for a girl to get fatter?'
jokes.setup: Amanda establishes their 'relationship' was purely professional
jokes.punchline: Jonah's explanation of bill-splitting logic: 'Why would I pay for a girl to get fatter?' — implying the food she'd eat on his dime would make her less attractive
jokes.explanation: The logic is disgusting and perfectly constructed — Jonah has an entire theory about restaurant economics and female attractiveness. The specificity ('get fatter') makes it funnier than a generic insult. Only Jonah could produce this reasoning.

jokes.text: Amanda on her relationship: 'I'm in a relationship with a good, normal man, who loves me. Yeah, normal.' — then: 'if he were to find out that we even had lunch together, I'm not sure he'd be strong enough to stand it.'
jokes.setup: Amanda explains why she needs Jonah to deny their connection
jokes.punchline: Her boyfriend is so fragile that knowledge of a single professional lunch with Jonah might destroy him
jokes.explanation: The triple emphasis on 'normal' ('a good, normal man... yeah, normal') implies her relief at having a non-Jonah. The joke about her boyfriend being unable to emotionally survive news of a lunch is a nice extension of the Jonah-as-contaminant theme.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'Jonah Ryan will not be silenced. We dated... and the world's gonna know that.'
jokes.setup: The NDA situation has inverted — Jonah is now the one refusing to deny a relationship
jokes.punchline: Jonah frames his refusal to sign a legal document as a heroic stand for truth and masculine pride — 'Jonah Ryan will not be silenced'
jokes.explanation: The third-person declaration ('Jonah Ryan will not be silenced') appropriates the language of activist resistance for the worst possible cause — a man's right to claim he dated someone who doesn't want to be associated with him. The inversion of #MeToo language is sharp and very of-its-moment.

jokes.text: Tom to Selina: 'I did everything I could to get Felix to give me the marshmallow speech.' / Selina: 'Did you offer to blow him?' / Tom: 'Tut-tut, Selina. We do not refer to Mr. Wade's sexual orientation. — But yes. Yes, I did.'
jokes.setup: Tom congratulates Selina on winning Felix's endorsement
jokes.punchline: Tom's mock-sanctimonious refusal to discuss Felix's sexuality — immediately followed by confirming he did offer sexual favors
jokes.explanation: A three-beat joke with excellent structure. Beat 1: 'Tut-tut, we do not refer to...' (mock propriety). Beat 2: pause. Beat 3: 'But yes. Yes, I did.' — the confirmation is funnier for the correction. The reversal from prim to admission in one sentence is very well-constructed. 'Tut-tut' is a perfect Tom James verbal tic.

jokes.text: 'Here's your marshmallow stick.' / 'Oh, that's very exciting, isn't it? OK.'
jokes.setup: Selina is about to give her big speech to Felix's assembled guests
jokes.punchline: The mundane physical prop — a marshmallow stick — is the symbol of her triumph, and her slightly manic enthusiasm about it ('Oh, that's very exciting, isn't it?') reveals her fragile excitement
jokes.explanation: A quiet character beat — the contrast between the stakes (presidential endorsement) and the prop (marshmallow stick) is the joke. Works but doesn't pop.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Well, I didn't tell him you were a lousy lay.' / Tom: 'Probably read it in our books.'
jokes.setup: Selina implies she helped Tom by not sharing negative information with Felix
jokes.punchline: Tom's response implies Selina has published this information — that her (and presumably his) memoirs contain references to their sexual history
jokes.explanation: The economy is excellent — 'Probably read it in our books' in five words implies: they had sex, it was bad, memoirs were written, the memoirs are public, Felix may have read them. Tom's casual deflation of Selina's 'favor' is beautifully dry. The line rewards political satire fans who understand the memoir-industrial complex.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I love you.' / Tom: 'I love you too, Tom. Go fuck yourself.'
jokes.setup: Tom says 'I love you' sincerely to Selina
jokes.punchline: Selina returns the sentiment and the dismissal in the same breath — 'I love you too, Tom. Go fuck yourself.' — as if they're standard closing words
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable moments. The seamless transition from 'I love you too' to 'go fuck yourself' without tonal shift is perfect Selina — love and hostility are the same register. The fact that she says his name mid-sentence ('I love you too, Tom') before the dismissal makes it warmer and colder simultaneously. Exceptional economy.

jokes.text: Watching Selina's incoherent marshmallow speech: 'She's having a textbook aneurysm.' / 'Still looks like a goddess.'
jokes.setup: Selina is visibly falling apart giving an impromptu speech
jokes.punchline: Gary's 'Still looks like a goddess' in response to a medical diagnosis is both his defining devotion and a complete non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: Gary's response is only funny coming from Gary — his unconditional worship of Selina, extending to neurological emergencies, is perfect character comedy. 'Textbook aneurysm' as a confident clinical assessment from a staffer is also funny in its deadpan authority. Together, the two lines are exceptional: clinical alarm meets pure devotional obliviousness.

jokes.text: Tom: 'Which I was all for it when it killed my dad, but it's not so funny when the shoe's on the other ventricle.'
jokes.setup: Tom reveals he had a quadruple bypass — his father died of the same condition
jokes.punchline: 'Not so funny when the shoe's on the other ventricle' — a riff on 'other foot' applied to cardiac anatomy
jokes.explanation: Among the episode's best pure wordplay. The phrase 'shoe's on the other foot' is familiar enough that 'ventricle' lands as a genuine surprise — and medically precise enough to seem clever rather than random. The dark prefix ('I was all for it when it killed my dad') makes the self-awareness funnier. This is a line that would work as stand-alone material. Exceptional originality and economy.

jokes.text: Selina reveals her heart attack was covered up as 'a routine prostate procedure.' Tom: 'Well, yeah, men have it so easy.'
jokes.setup: Tom reveals his bypass; Selina reveals her covered-up heart attack
jokes.punchline: The cover story used was 'a routine prostate procedure' — anatomically impossible for a woman. Tom's 'men have it so easy' acknowledges the absurdity with dry sympathy
jokes.explanation: The political observation embedded in the joke is sharp: a female politician covering up a heart attack with a male-coded medical procedure — because a female heart attack would be more politically damaging than a routine male procedure. Tom's dry acknowledgment is perfectly calibrated. Efficient, pointed, and funny.

jokes.text: Selina: 'People assumed I was having a face-lift.' / Tom: 'When I had my face-lift, we told people that you were having a face-lift.'
jokes.setup: Both reveal various health cover-ups; Selina mentions the face-lift misassumption
jokes.punchline: Tom reveals he had a face-lift AND used Selina as his cover story — meaning her name was associated with a face-lift she wasn't having, to conceal his
jokes.explanation: Structurally brilliant. The escalation takes Selina's misassumption and reveals it was actively manufactured by Tom — meaning the cover-up cover-up used her face as the excuse. The double-layered vanity (both had face-lifts, both needed cover) and the betrayal (he used her face as his alibi) are compressed into one line. The rhythm is excellent.

jokes.text: Tom imagines an alternate life: 'And we argue about how much money we're spending on landscaping, and I think you flirt a little too much with the kids' tennis coach. And then we blow each other's brains out.'
jokes.setup: Tom romantically imagines an alternate universe where they're unhappily married in the suburbs
jokes.punchline: The suburban marital bickering (landscaping, tennis coach flirting) escalates to mutual murder — 'blow each other's brains out' — delivered as if it's the natural endpoint of suburban life
jokes.explanation: The escalation from mundane suburban detail (landscaping, tennis coach) to mutual homicide is perfectly paced. The 'assistant manager' detail earlier makes the suburban life specific and sad. Tom delivering 'blow each other's brains out' as the natural marital endpoint — and Selina's 'Yeah' of agreement — is both funny and melancholy.

jokes.text: Selina: 'And you work at a car dealership?' / Tom: 'Assistant manager.' / Selina: 'Oh! Look at you!'
jokes.setup: Tom is imagining their alternate suburban life
jokes.punchline: Selina's genuine enthusiasm for 'assistant manager' — the way she celebrates the downgrade as an achievement — captures both characters' complicated relationship with power and status
jokes.explanation: 'Oh! Look at you!' — Selina treating 'assistant manager of a car dealership' as if it's a real achievement is a perfect character beat. It's both sincere (she's briefly imagining pride in a small victory) and absurd (given they're running for president).

jokes.text: Gary: 'How about a hot soak with a Laura Mercier bath bomb?' / Selina: 'Is it gonna explode between my legs and make me cum until I cry?' / Gary: 'I think it's peppermint.'
jokes.setup: After her humiliation, Gary tries to comfort Selina with a bath
jokes.punchline: Selina describes in explicit terms what she needs from a bath bomb; Gary's response 'I think it's peppermint' treats the inquiry as a product specification question
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments. Structure is perfect: Gary's innocent setup, Selina's explicit and emotionally raw question, Gary's completely literal product-information answer. 'I think it's peppermint' is one of the best straight-man responses in the series — Gary's inability to engage with the emotional content while being meticulously helpful about bath product ingredients is both deeply funny and quietly heartbreaking. The line is also the most explicit possible expression of the Selina/Gary dynamic.

jokes.text: Dan's speech on meaningless sex: 'You pull it out to cum on her tits 'cause you think it's gonna make you feel alive. But it doesn't matter where you cum, Ames. Ya know? It just doesn't.'
jokes.setup: Dan is reflecting on meaningless sex as a substitute for meaning
jokes.punchline: The philosophical conclusion — 'it doesn't matter where you cum' — delivered with deep existential weight as if it's a profound life lesson
jokes.explanation: Dan delivering this as sincere middle-age philosophy to the pregnant woman he impregnated and refused is both deeply funny and deeply sad. 'It doesn't matter where you cum, Ames' as a piece of existential wisdom is exactly the right level of ridiculous profundity. The scene is structured beautifully: Dan's crisis, his graphic description, and his profound conclusion — all in total unawareness that Amy is carrying his child.

jokes.text: Amy's reaction shot/silence during and after Dan's speech — cut to her calling an OB/GYN for an abortion
jokes.setup: Dan has just given a deeply moving (to himself) speech about not knowing what he'd do without Amy and 'the future'
jokes.punchline: The immediate cut to Amy scheduling an abortion — the juxtaposition is the joke
jokes.explanation: Exceptional structural comedy. Dan's speech ends on 'The future...' with genuine emotion — the cut to Amy scheduling the abortion is the sharpest possible rebuttal. The economy is extraordinary: no dialogue needed. The audience does the work. Dan's profundity meets Amy's pragmatism in the most devastating possible way. Strong 8.8.

jokes.text: Selina on Kemi and Tom: 'They could reassure people that look like him that their country wouldn't be ruined by people that look like her.'
jokes.setup: Selina sees Tom and Kemi together and imagines them as a ticket
jokes.punchline: Selina's characteristically racist-coded political analysis of an interracial political ticket is delivered as strategic assessment
jokes.explanation: Pointed and uncomfortable satire. Selina's framing of racial diversity as a 'reassurance device' for white voters ('people that look like him') is both an accurate description of how some political strategists think AND deeply condemning of Selina's worldview. The euphemistic structure — 'look like him/look like her' — makes the casual racism more devastating. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: Felix on stopping cocaine: 'I'm addicted to disruption, especially since I stopped using cocaine.' / Selina and aides stare in silence.
jokes.setup: Felix warmly embraces Selina's 'disruption' pitch
jokes.punchline: His enthusiastic endorsement of disruption is immediately explained by his recent cocaine sobriety — disruption is his new addiction
jokes.explanation: The cocaine admission delivered casually as a subordinate clause ('especially since I stopped using cocaine') is very Veep. The fact that Selina doesn't respond — just stares — is the ideal reaction beat. Felix's offhand drug history as a character detail enriches the satire of the donor class.

jokes.text: Selina credits Felix for the running mate idea: 'You gave me the idea.' / Felix: 'I'm not shocked about that.'
jokes.setup: Selina is crediting Felix with her own idea using the repeat-back tactic
jokes.punchline: Felix's 'I'm not shocked about that' — he genuinely believes his ideas come through people without their awareness
jokes.explanation: A payoff to the Felix vanity gag. His unshocked acceptance of having psychically transmitted the idea is a good character beat — his narcissism has a quasi-mystical dimension.

jokes.text: Richard's speech: 'A lot of you knew me when I was really little and I was just running around in diapers. And now here we are, and a lot of you are wearin' diapers.'
jokes.setup: Richard addresses a presumably older crowd with a nostalgic opener
jokes.punchline: The parallel structure inverts from childhood to geriatric incontinence — 'and a lot of you are wearin' diapers' — delivered with complete warmth
jokes.explanation: Richard's delivery is everything here. The parallel structure (diapers as a child / diapers as elderly) is a genuinely elegant joke construction, made funnier by the warmth with which he says it — he means it as a compliment. Richard's obliviousness is his defining comedic trait, and this is a perfect expression of it.

jokes.text: Jonah: 'There was nothing professional about my behavior! — Nothing happened, Mr. Ryan. — I'm in a relationship with a good, normal man...'
jokes.setup: Amanda is giving her 'denial' press statement; Jonah interrupts to claim wrongdoing
jokes.punchline: Jonah argues against his own defense — interrupting to insist he WAS inappropriate — while Amanda continues to deny
jokes.explanation: A beautifully sustained structural joke. Amanda is denying everything to protect herself; Jonah is publicly insisting he was 'all over her' and 'got super handsy' to protect his ego. The two people are defending completely opposite positions for completely self-interested reasons, and they're talking past each other in a press conference. Perfect Veep structural comedy.

jokes.text: Amanda: 'His behavior was completely appropriate at all times.' / Jonah: 'Ohh, no! I was all over her! I got super handsy! For too long women have been silent...'
jokes.setup: Amanda exonerates Jonah on live TV
jokes.punchline: Jonah, watching at home, argues against his own exoneration — insisting he WAS inappropriate, volunteering the information, framing his admission in #MeToo language
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke. It is a rare television moment where every comedic element works simultaneously. The structural inversion is perfect: Jonah is arguing against exoneration. 'I was all over her! I got super handsy!' delivered with apparent pride. Then 'For too long women have been silent' — co-opting feminist language to defend his own harassment. The joke works satirically (it skewers both performative feminism AND Jonah's narcissism), structurally (the callback to the NDA scene pays off perfectly), and characteristically (only Jonah Ryan could do this). This is 'Snip, snap' tier. The 9.2 craft is earned.

jokes.text: Felix approves the all-female ticket: 'An all-female ticket? The first one since Carter-Mondale. I'm not mad at that.'
jokes.setup: Selina introduces Kemi as potential running mate and pitches the all-female ticket
jokes.punchline: Carter-Mondale was not an all-female ticket — Walter Mondale was male. Felix's misremembering of recent political history while pretending authority is the joke
jokes.explanation: Felix's confident misattribution of history (Carter-Mondale was two men; presumably he's thinking of Mondale-Ferraro in 1984) is a sharp character joke — the billionaire donor who controls the political process doesn't actually know political history. It's either a deliberate misremembering or genuine ignorance, and either reading is funny.

jokes.text: Dan tells Amy he's about to have sex with a 19-year-old cater waitress named Meagan — while Amy has just been emotionally processing whether to have his baby.
jokes.setup: Amy has been working up to saying something significant about the baby
jokes.punchline: Dan interrupts to announce a hookup — then adds 'And get this. Her name's Meagan.' — as if the name is the twist
jokes.explanation: The punchline is delivered across two beats: the 19-year-old cater waitress (oblivious cruelty) and then 'And get this. Her name's Meagan.' — the name somehow being the thing Dan thinks is interesting. Dan's complete blindness to what Amy is going through is perfect, and 'Oh-hhhhh...' from Amy is a devastating reaction beat.

jokes.text: Amy calls to schedule an abortion: 'Yes, it's for an abortion. Yes, I'm sure. Oh, do you also do flu shots?'
jokes.setup: After Dan's oblivious announcement, Amy calls an OB/GYN
jokes.punchline: The detached efficiency of 'Yes, I'm sure' (preempting judgment) followed immediately by 'Oh, do you also do flu shots?' — treating the abortion as a routine errand to bundle with other medical tasks
jokes.explanation: Among the very best jokes in the episode. The structure is exceptional: the weight of the decision, Selina's earlier pressure, Dan's obliviousness — all building to Amy calling with complete pragmatic efficiency. 'Yes, I'm sure' preempts the condescending pause she clearly expects. Then 'Oh, do you also do flu shots?' makes the appointment a convenience errand. It's funny, feminist, dark, and completely in character. The flu shot request is perfectly specific — she's making it a one-stop appointment. Comparable to anchor-level economy.

jokes.text: Ben: 'You're gonna be drowning in money so dark it could get shot entering its own apartment.'
jokes.setup: Ben is excitedly telling Selina about Felix's campaign donation
jokes.punchline: The description of the money as 'so dark it could get shot entering its own apartment' — comparing the shadiness of the donation to racial profiling
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly constructed extended metaphor. Describing dark money using racial profiling imagery is both culturally specific and sharp — it draws a line between political donor corruption and systemic racism. The specificity ('entering its own apartment') grounds the metaphor. Ben, as always, delivers the most devastating observations with complete calm. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Seriously, it's America's first two-cooter ticket. Look great on a button.'
jokes.setup: Selina celebrates the all-female ticket announcement
jokes.punchline: 'Two-cooter ticket' as a coinage for a female presidential ticket — Selina reducing historic feminist progress to a vulgar branding exercise
jokes.explanation: 'Two-cooter ticket' is an original coinage that only Selina would use — she simultaneously celebrates a feminist milestone and degrades it. 'Look great on a button' as the key selling point further reduces it to merchandise. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: On Tom sleeping with his aide: 'How dare that smooth shitsack cheat on his wife and risk his political future with someone that's not me?'
jokes.setup: Selina learns Tom is sleeping with his Deputy Chief of Staff
jokes.punchline: Selina's outrage is entirely about being bypassed — her objection to the affair is that she wasn't the affair partner
jokes.explanation: Among the episode's top three jokes. The structure is perfect: 'How dare that smooth shitsack cheat on his wife' sounds like righteous moral outrage — then 'with someone that's not me' reveals the entire basis of her anger. It's not the betrayal or the hypocrisy; it's the exclusion. This joke contains Selina's entire character in one sentence. Exceptional character integration, earned humor, and perfect economy. 'Smooth shitsack' is also a remarkable compound insult.

jokes.text: Felix Wade outing discussion: 'Felix Wade is gay? / Thank you, ma'am!'
jokes.setup: Selina has accidentally outed Felix Wade after months of the team discretely managing the secret
jokes.punchline: One aide, apparently unaware despite the entire team knowing, reacts with surprise — then another thanks Selina for confirming what they all knew
jokes.explanation: A functional payoff to the Felix secret. The mix of reactions (one surprised, one grateful) is a decent ensemble beat, but it's more plot-functional than comic.

jokes.text: Jonah gets good poll news from the #NotMe movement. Aide's phone is upside-down; reads 'EWtoN' — then corrects: 'It's still NotMe. — You should lock your screen, dude.'
jokes.setup: Jonah's aide presents upside-down polling data
jokes.punchline: The hashtag '#NotMe' becomes '#EWtoN' upside down — and then it's still just NotMe right-side up, the error being purely mechanical
jokes.explanation: A competent visual gag. The upside-down phone revealing nonsense is physically funny; 'You should lock your screen, dude' as the corrective response is a decent button. Functional rather than exceptional.

jokes.text: Ben on Mike: 'Where is that fat-faced, freckled fuck-it-up-agus? — Hiding in the bathroom, ma'am, making pretend diarrhea noises.'
jokes.setup: Mike has published the article outing Felix; Selina is furious
jokes.punchline: 'Fat-faced, freckled fuck-it-up-agus' as an alliterative insult portmanteau, followed by the specific intelligence that he's making pretend diarrhea noises in the bathroom
jokes.explanation: 'Fuck-it-up-agus' is an inspired coinage — combining 'fuck it up' with '-agus' (Greek anatomical suffix, as in esophagus) to create a pseudo-medical term for a habitual ruiner. The alliteration (fat-faced, freckled, fuck-it-up) gives it comedic rhythm. The follow-up image of Mike making pretend diarrhea noises in a bathroom stall is both specific and perfectly cowardly. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I just can't believe that that second-hand Muppet wrote an article that people actually read.'
jokes.setup: Mike's BuzzFeed article has caused a crisis
jokes.punchline: 'Second-hand Muppet' as an insult — implying Mike is not even a first-generation Muppet, but a used/inferior version
jokes.explanation: 'Second-hand Muppet' is a great insult — the 'Muppet' (British slang for an idiot) modified by 'second-hand' to imply he's a defective/used version of an already low standard. It also has the Veep quality of being slightly inexplicable, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Selina: 'I'm gonna choke him with that giant cock of his, see how he likes it.'
jokes.setup: Selina is furious at Felix's demotion
jokes.punchline: A vulgar threat that's absurdist in its logic — she's going to weaponize his own anatomy against him
jokes.explanation: A functional Selina outrage line with good vulgarity. Not among the episode's sharpest — the joke relies mostly on shock — but 'see how he likes it' as a tag is a decent addition.

jokes.text: 'Hiding in the bathroom, ma'am, making pretend diarrhea noises.'
jokes.setup: Selina asks where Mike is
jokes.punchline: The exact specificity of 'pretend diarrhea noises' — not hiding, not sick, but performing gastrointestinal distress as cover
jokes.explanation: The 'pretend' qualifier is everything — Mike isn't sick, he's performing sickness for an audience. The specificity of 'diarrhea noises' (not just vague sounds) is perfect Mike characterization. A very funny single line.

jokes.text: Aide suggests an outfit; Selina says Felix 'doesn't give a shit. Just because Felix Wade is gayer than an Eames chair in assless leather chaps doesn't mean that he's some sort of fashion diva.'
jokes.setup: An aide suggests fashion considerations for meeting Felix
jokes.punchline: 'Gayer than an Eames chair in assless leather chaps' — a design-world specific simile for maximum gayness
jokes.explanation: Exceptional simile. 'Eames chair' specifically invokes mid-century modern design culture, itself coded gay in a particular register, before adding 'assless leather chaps.' The combination is absurdly specific and highly original. The observation that this doesn't make Felix a 'fashion diva' adds a secondary joke about not conflating gay identity with fashion knowledge. Among the episode's most creative lines.

jokes.text: Michelle to Selina: 'I'm the Senator's Deputy Chief of Staff.' / Selina: 'Congratulations. Then you probably won't fuck it up.'
jokes.setup: Michelle identifies her title in response to Selina's attack
jokes.punchline: 'Congratulations. Then you probably won't fuck it up.' — acknowledging the title while damning it with faint, sexually loaded praise
jokes.explanation: The line works on two levels: genuine contempt and a reference to the sexual implication of 'fucking' (she did fuck her way to the position, per Selina's earlier implication). 'Probably won't' is the precise word — not 'definitely,' but 'probably,' maintaining the insult.

jokes.text: Selina: 'God, I feel so sorry for whoever's stickin' their dick in that bag of mayonnaise.'
jokes.setup: After her confrontation with Michelle, Selina walks away
jokes.punchline: 'Bag of mayonnaise' as a description of a human being — the specific culinary/texture insult
jokes.explanation: Memorably specific insult. The image of a 'bag of mayonnaise' as sexual metaphor is both disgusting and precise — somehow more damning than a generic insult. Selina's false sympathy ('feel so sorry for') makes it more cutting.

jokes.text: Tom on Felix's donation: 'Sadly, I am not the recipient of Mr. Wade's munificence.' / Selina: 'Wha...' / Tom: 'Well, he sure as shit didn't pick me.' — then reveals Kemi
jokes.setup: Selina expects Tom is the one who got Felix's endorsement
jokes.punchline: Neither Selina nor Tom got it — Felix chose Kemi, Selina's 'protégée' who she just met this weekend
jokes.explanation: A well-structured plot-comedy reveal. The callback to the invented protégée pays off nicely — Kemi, whom Selina just met and has no relationship with, is the one who benefited from Selina's accidental outing of Felix.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Fuck me in the Aspen.' / 'The tri-racial twat stabbed me in the back!' / Tom: 'She really is your protégée.'
jokes.setup: Selina learns Kemi got Felix's endorsement
jokes.punchline: 'Fuck me in the Aspen' (location-specific expletive); 'tri-racial twat' (Selina's assessment of Kemi); Tom's 'She really is your protégée' — because Kemi has now successfully stabbed someone in the back
jokes.explanation: Three-joke sequence that builds to a perfect button. 'Fuck me in the Aspen' is the episode's second location-specific expletive (callback to 'Rocky Mountain fuck'). 'Tri-racial twat' is Selina's casually monstrous characterization. But Tom's 'She really is your protégée' is the episode's best callback — because by backstabbing Selina, Kemi has literally become what Selina trained her (without knowing her) to be. It retroactively validates the fake mentor relationship. Exceptional structural comedy.

jokes.text: 'The tri-racial twat stabbed me in the back!' / 'She really is your protégée.'
jokes.setup: Kemi Talbot, whom Selina claimed as her protégée all episode, has just taken Felix's support
jokes.punchline: Tom's 'She really is your protégée' — Kemi's betrayal proves she learned from Selina, who has backstabbed everyone
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback payoff. 'She really is your protégée' works because it validates the protégée relationship Selina kept asserting — but the evidence is Kemi's betrayal, not her success. Tom administering this observation with quiet precision while Selina is in rage is excellent. The structure pays off one of the episode's running threads completely.

jokes.text: Richard to Selina: 'It's so nice to be back home in Iowa.' / Selina: 'Place is a dump.'
jokes.setup: Selina has just suffered a catastrophic defeat; Richard provides folksy warmth
jokes.punchline: Selina's single-sentence destruction of Iowa as a political locale — and of Richard's sincerity
jokes.explanation: A clean Selina line — her unfiltered contempt for everywhere she has to campaign is a reliable character beat. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Richard says Selina's polls are 'way up' after leaving — Selina: 'Maybe I should just stop campaigning altogether.' / Richard: 'Couldn't hurt.'
jokes.setup: Her numbers improved after she left New Hampshire
jokes.punchline: Richard's earnest 'Couldn't hurt' treating Selina's joke as a genuine policy proposal is a perfect Richard beat
jokes.explanation: Richard's sincerity in agreeing with a sarcastic suggestion is a reliable character joke. 'Couldn't hurt' is delivered with the helpful neutrality of someone who has genuinely considered it.

jokes.text: Selina: 'Oh, remember, lift with your back.' / Richard: 'It's your legs. L-Legs.' / Selina: 'No, you lift with your back.' / Richard: 'Oh. That's incorrect.' / Selina: 'OK, well. That could probably be the main cause of your scoliosis.' / Richard: 'I have scoliosis?'
jokes.setup: Selina sends Richard to get boxes from the car and gives him deliberately wrong lifting advice
jokes.punchline: The escalating disagreement — Selina insisting the wrong method, Richard correcting her gently, Selina blaming Richard's scoliosis on his correct technique, Richard not knowing he has scoliosis
jokes.explanation: Perfect closing joke. Four-beat escalation: wrong advice, gentle correction, blaming the correct method, medical diagnosis revelation. 'I have scoliosis?' as the final line is ideal — Richard's surprised acceptance of a new medical condition is both sad and funny. The bit also captures Selina's petty power plays and Richard's inability to push back effectively. Strong ending to the episode.

tmdb_overview: At an Aspen retreat for rich donors, Selina deals with potential adversaries and allies. Amy's behavior raises Gary's suspicions.
insight: Relentless escalation drives 87 jokes in 35 minutes—Veep's cringe machinery at full throttle.
